question_text: If a line makes an angle of 30 o with the positive direction of x-axis, 120 o with the positive direction of y-axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of z-axis is:

options.explanation: To find the angle with the z-axis, we use the relation: {tex} \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1{/tex} where {tex}\alpha=30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=120^{\circ}{/tex} . Calculating, we get: {tex} \cos ^2 30^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}, \cos ^2 120^{\circ}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \Longrightarrow \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \Longrightarrow \gamma=90^{\circ} .{/tex} So, the angle with the z-axis is 90 o .

question_html: Find the distance of a point (2, 4, –1) from the line {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the distance of a point (2, 4, –1) from the line {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}}{/tex} .
explanation: We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} , then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} . Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9. Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3). {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}
solution: We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} , then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} . Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9. Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3). {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}
solution_html:
We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex}.
{tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex}
Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex}, then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex}.
Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9.
Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line.
{tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3).
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL{tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Two motorcycles A and B are running at the speed more than allowed speed on the road along the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}, respectively.

  1. Find the cartesian equation of the line along which motorcycle A is running. (1)
  2. Find the direction cosines of line along which motorcycle A is running. (1)
  3. Find the direction ratios of line along which motorcycle B is running. (2)
    OR
    Find the shortest distance between the given lines. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Two motorcycles A and B are running at the speed more than allowed speed on the road along the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} , respectively. Find the cartesian equation of the line along which motorcycle A is running. (1) Find the direction cosines of line along which motorcycle A is running. (1) Find the direction ratios of line along which motorcycle B is running. (2) OR Find the shortest distance between the given lines. (2)
explanation: The line along which motorcycle A is running, {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} , which can be rewritten as {tex}(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \lambda \hat{j}-\lambda \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\lambda{/tex} , y = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , z = - {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\lambda, \frac{y}{2}=\lambda, \frac{z}{-1}=\lambda{/tex} Thus, the required cartesian equation is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} Clearly, D.R.'s of the required line are < 1, 2, -1 > {tex}\therefore{/tex} D.C.'s are {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}){/tex} i.e., {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}){/tex} The line along which motorcycle B is running, is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} , which is parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} D.R.'s of the required line are ( 2, 1, 1 ). OR Here, {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 9 - 9 = 0 Hence, shortest distance between the given lines is 0.
solution: The line along which motorcycle A is running, {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} , which can be rewritten as {tex}(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \lambda \hat{j}-\lambda \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\lambda{/tex} , y = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , z = - {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\lambda, \frac{y}{2}=\lambda, \frac{z}{-1}=\lambda{/tex} Thus, the required cartesian equation is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} Clearly, D.R.'s of the required line are < 1, 2, -1 > {tex}\therefore{/tex} D.C.'s are {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}){/tex} i.e., {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}){/tex} The line along which motorcycle B is running, is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} , which is parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} D.R.'s of the required line are ( 2, 1, 1 ). OR Here, {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 9 - 9 = 0 Hence, shortest distance between the given lines is 0.
solution_html:
1. The line along which motorcycle A is running, {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}, which can be rewritten as {tex}(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=\lambda \hat{i}+2 \lambda \hat{j}-\lambda \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\lambda{/tex}, y = 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}, z = -{tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\lambda, \frac{y}{2}=\lambda, \frac{z}{-1}=\lambda{/tex}
    Thus, the required cartesian equation is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex}

  2. Clearly, D.R.'s of the required line are < 1, 2, -1 >

    {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}){/tex}
    i.e., {tex}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}){/tex}

explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 3}&2 \\ 2&3&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec b_1×\vec b_2|=\sqrt {(-9)^2+3^2+9^2}\\ =\sqrt{3^2}\sqrt{3^2+1+3^2}=3\sqrt{19}{/tex} Required shortest distance {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\left|\frac{(a_2-a_1).(b_2-b_1)}{\vert b_1\times b_2\vert}\right|=\left|\frac{-9\times3+3\times3+9\times3}{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\left|\frac9{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\frac3{\sqrt{19}}units\\\end{array}{/tex}

solution_html:

question_text: Find the perpendicular distance of the point (1, 0, 0) from the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} . Also, find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular and the equation of the perpendicular.
explanation: Suppose the point (1, 0, 0) be P and the point through which the line passes be Q(1,-1,-10). The line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{P Q}=0 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b} \times \overrightarrow{P Q}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -3 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & -10 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}38 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|=\sqrt{38^{2}+20^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1444+400+4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1848}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{1848}}{\sqrt{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{24}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{6}{/tex} Suppose L be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(1,0,0) to the given line- The coordinates of a general point on the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} are given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+1{/tex} {tex}y=-3 \lambda-1{/tex} {tex}z=8 \lambda-10{/tex} Suppose the coordinates of L be {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} Since,The direction ratios of PL are proportional to, {tex}2 \lambda+1-1,-3 \lambda-1-0{/tex} , {tex}8 \lambda-10-0{/tex} , i.e., {tex}2 \lambda,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10{/tex} Since,The direction ratios of the given line are proportional to 2, -3, 8, but PL is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2(2 \lambda)-3(-3 \lambda-1)+8(8 \lambda-10){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} we get the coordinates of L as (3, -4, -2). explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + \hat j,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 1}&1 \\ 3&{ - 5}&2 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat i (-2+5)-\hat j(4-3)+\hat k(-10+3){/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i - \hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|=\sqrt {9+1+49}= \sqrt {59} {/tex} Also, {tex}(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)=(3\hat i-\hat j-7 \hat k)(\hat i-\hat k)=3+7+0=10{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)}{|\vec b_1×\vec b_2|}\right|=\frac{10}{\sqrt{59}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-9}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+15}{-7}=\frac{z-9}{5}{/tex}
explanation: Here,it is given equations of lines {tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{-3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+15}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{5}{/tex} Direction ratios of L 1 and L 2 are (2, 1, -3) are (2, -7, 5) respectively. Suppose, general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = 2s - 1, y 1 = s + 1, z 1 = -3s + 9 and let general point on line L 2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2, z 2) x 2 = 2t + 3, y 2 = -7t - 15, z 2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get {tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex} {tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex} {tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, 2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and 2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and 10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132 Solving above two equations, we obtain t = -2 and s = 2 thus, P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1) Now, distance between points P and Q is {tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z

Suppose, general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = 2s - 1, y 1 = s + 1, z 1 = -3s + 9 and let general point on line L 2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2, z 2) x 2 = 2t + 3, y 2 = -7t - 15, z 2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get {tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex} {tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex} {tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, 2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and 2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and 10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132 Solving above two equations, we obtain t = -2 and s = 2 thus, P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1) Now, distance between points P and Q is {tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z
solution_html:
Here,it is given equations of lines
{tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+15}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{5}{/tex}
Direction ratios of L1 and L2 are (2, 1, -3) are (2, -7, 5) respectively.
Suppose, general point on line L1 is P = (x1, y1, z1)
x1 = 2s - 1, y1 = s + 1, z1 = -3s + 9
and let general point on line L2 is Q = (x2, y2, z2)
x2 = 2t + 3, y2 = -7t - 15, z2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get
{tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex}{tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex}{tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s))
PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines
Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity,
2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and
2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and
10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132
Solving above two equations, we obtain
t = -2 and s = 2
thus,
P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1)
Now, distance between points P and Q is
{tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units
Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z
Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
explanation: We have lines {tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex} Any point on the line L 1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} Any point on the line L 2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation. Thus lines intersect. Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).
solution: We have lines {tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex} Any point on the line L 1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} Any point on the line L 2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation. Thus lines intersect. Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).
solution_html:
We have lines
{tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex}
Any point on the line L1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex}
Any point on the line L2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex}
These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation.
Thus lines intersect.
Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).

question_text: Find the foot of the perpendicular drawn from point {tex}(2,-1,5){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{{x}-11}{10}=\frac{{y}+2}{-4}=\frac{{z}+8}{-11}{/tex} . Also, find the length of the perpendicular.
explanation: Let: {tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex} Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11 Direction ratio of the perpendicular is {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex} Since this is perpendicular to the line, {tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex} {tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.
solution: Let: {tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex} Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11 Direction ratio of the perpendicular is {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex} Since this is perpendicular to the line, {tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex} {tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.
solution_html:
Let: 
{tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex}
So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex}
Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11
Direction ratio of the perpendicular is
{tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex}{tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex}{tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex}
Since this is perpendicular to the line,
{tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex}{tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex}{tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex}
So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3)
Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units
Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.

question_text: Find the image of the point (5, 9, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex}
explanation: Suppose, {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} The direction ratios of the perpendicular is {tex}(2 \lambda+1-5):(3 \lambda+2-9){/tex} {tex}:(4 \lambda+3-3){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(2 \lambda-4):(3 \lambda-7):(4 \lambda){/tex} Direction ratio of the line is 2 : 3 : 4 From the direction ratio of the line and the direct ratio of its perpendicular, we have {tex}2(2 \lambda-4)+3(3 \lambda-7)+4(4 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \lambda-8+9 \lambda-21+16 \lambda=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 29 \lambda=29{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (3, 5, 7) The foot of the perpendicular is the mid-point of the line joining (5, 9, 3) and {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} Therefore, we have {tex}\frac{\alpha+5}{2}=3 \Rightarrow \alpha=1{/tex} {tex}\frac{\beta+9}{2}=5 \Rightarrow \beta=1{/tex} {tex}\frac{y+3}{2}=7 \Rightarrow \gamma=11{/tex} Therefore, the image is (1, 1, 11)

solution_html:
Suppose,
{tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex}
The direction ratios of the perpendicular is
{tex}(2 \lambda+1-5):(3 \lambda+2-9){/tex}{tex}:(4 \lambda+3-3){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(2 \lambda-4):(3 \lambda-7):(4 \lambda){/tex}
Direction ratio of the line is 2 : 3 : 4

From the direction ratio of the line and the direct ratio of its perpendicular, we have
{tex}2(2 \lambda-4)+3(3 \lambda-7)+4(4 \lambda)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \lambda-8+9 \lambda-21+16 \lambda=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 29 \lambda=29{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}

The foot of the perpendicular is the mid-point of the line joining (5, 9, 3) and {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex}
Therefore, we have
{tex}\frac{\alpha+5}{2}=3 \Rightarrow \alpha=1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\beta+9}{2}=5 \Rightarrow \beta=1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{y+3}{2}=7 \Rightarrow \gamma=11{/tex}
Therefore, the image is (1, 1, 11)

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{k})+{/tex}{tex}\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}intersect. Also, find their point intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{k})+{/tex} {tex}\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} intersect. Also, find their point intersection.
explanation: Here,it is given that {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{k})+{/tex} {tex}\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\imath}(12-9)-\hat{\jmath}(4-6)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(3-4){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+4+1}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(2-2) \hat{1}+{/tex} {tex}(6-0) \hat{\jmath}+(3+3) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \times 0)+(2 \times 6){/tex} {tex}+((-1) \times 6){/tex} = 0 + 12 - 6 = 6 Thus, the shortest distance between the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units As {tex}d \neq 0{/tex} , the given lines do not intersect.
solution: Here,it is given that {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{k})+{/tex} {tex}\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{\imath}(12-9)-\hat{\jmath}(4-6)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(3-4){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+4+1}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(2-2) \hat{1}+{/tex} {tex}(6-0) \hat{\jmath}+(3+3) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}=(3 \times 0)+(2 \times 6){/tex} {tex}+((-1) \times 6){/tex} = 0 + 12 - 6 = 6 Thus, the shortest distance between the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units As {tex}d \neq 0{/tex} , the given lines do not intersect.
solution_html:
Here,it is given that
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{k})+{/tex}{tex}\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
Here,
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{\imath}(12-9)-\hat{\jmath}(4-6)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(3-4){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{9+4+1}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(2-2) \hat{1}+{/tex}{tex}(6-0) \hat{\jmath}+(3+3) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex}{tex}=(3 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(0 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}=(3 \times 0)+(2 \times 6){/tex}{tex}+((-1) \times 6){/tex}
= 0 + 12 - 6
= 6
Thus, the shortest distance between the given lines is
{tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d=\frac{6}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units
As {tex}d \neq 0{/tex}
, the given lines do not intersect.

Here,it is given that
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} 
Here,
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=i+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=4 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}(3-8)-\hat{j}(2-20){/tex}{tex}+\hat{k}(4-15){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+18 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-11 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+18^{2}+(-11)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{25+324+121}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{470}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(4-1) \hat{i}{/tex}{tex}+(1-2) \hat{\jmath}+(0-3) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now,we have
{tex}(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})={/tex}{tex}(-5 \hat{i}+18 \hat{j}-11 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=((-5) \times 3)+(18 \times{/tex}{tex}(-1))+((-11) \times(-3)){/tex}
= -15 - 18 + 33
= 0
Thus, the distance between the given lines is
{tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{0}{\sqrt{470}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} d = 0 units
As d = 0
Thus, the given lines intersect each other.
Now, to find a point of intersection, let us convert given vector equations into Cartesian equations.
For that putting {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} in given equations,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{L}_1: \mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}={/tex}{tex}(i+2 j+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 i+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{L}_2: \mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}={/tex} {tex}(4 \hat{i}+\hat{\jmath})+\mu(5 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{L}_1:(\mathrm{x}-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mathrm{y}-2) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+{/tex}{tex}(z-3) \hat{k}=2 \lambda \hat{\imath}+3 \lambda \hat{\jmath}+4 \lambda \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{L}_2:(\mathrm{x}-4) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mathrm{y}-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+{/tex}{tex}(z-0) \hat{k}=5 \mu \hat{\imath}+2 \mu \hat{\jmath}+\mu \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{3}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{z-0}{1}=\mu{/tex}
General point on L1 is
x1 = 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1, y1 = 3{tex}\lambda{/tex}+ 2, z1 = 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3
Suppose, P(x1,  y1, z1) be point of intersection of two given lines.
Thus, point P satisfies the equation of line {tex}\vec{L}_2{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{2 \lambda+1-4}{5}=\frac{3 \lambda+2-1}{2}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{4 \lambda+3-0}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{2 \lambda-3}{5}=\frac{3 \lambda+1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 \lambda-6=15 \lambda+5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 11 \lambda=-11{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex}
Thus, x1 = 2(-1) + 1, y1 = 3(-1) + 2, z1 = 4(-1) + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = -1, y1 = -1, z1 = -1
Therefore, point of intersection of given lines is (-1, -1, -1).

question_html:
Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex}.
Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex} . Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
explanation: We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex} , then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex} . Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line. {tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2). Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
solution: We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex} , then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex} . Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line. {tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2). Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
solution_html:
We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex}

Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex}, then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex}.
Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line.
{tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2).

Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}

solution_html:
Given Cartesian equations of lines
{tex}\mathrm{L}_1=\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{5}{/tex}
Line L1 is passing through point (1, -1, 1) and has direction ratios (3, 2, 5)
Thus, vector equation of line L1 is
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
And
{tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-2}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{-2}{/tex}
Line L2 is passing through point (2, -1, 1) and has direction ratios (2, 3, -2)
Thus, vector equation of line L2 is
{tex}\vec{\mathbf{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}
Now, to calculate distance between the lines,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathbf{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}
Here,we have
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=1-j+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 & -2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}(-4-15)-\hat{j}(-6-10)+\hat{k}(9-4){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=-19 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+16 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|=\sqrt{(-19)^{2}+16^{2}+5^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{361+256+25}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{642}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(2-1) \hat{i}+(1+1) \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+(-1-1) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=1+2 \hat{\jmath}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})=(-19 \hat{1}+16 \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}=((-19) \times 1)+(16 \times 2)+(5 \times(-2)){/tex}
= -19 + 32 - 10
= 3
Thus, the shortest distance between the given lines is
{tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{3}{\sqrt{642}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{642}}{/tex} units
As {tex}d \neq 0{/tex}
Hence, given lines do not intersect each other.

question_text: Find the shortest distance between the given lines. {tex}\vec{r}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} , {tex}\vec{r}=(-9 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Here,it is given that {tex}\vec{r}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(-9 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Here,we have {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=6 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=-9 i+j-10 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=4 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 4 \\ 4 & 1 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=i(-6-4)-j(12-16){/tex} {tex}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2+4){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{100+16+36}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{152}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(-9-6) \hat{1}{/tex} {tex}+(1-0) \hat{\jmath}+(6-3) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})={/tex} {tex}(-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=((-10) \times(-15)){/tex} {tex}+(4 \times 1)+(6 \times 3){/tex} = 150 + 4 + 18 = 172 Thus, the shortest distance the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{172}{\sqrt{152}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{172}{2 \sqrt{38}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{86}{\sqrt{38}}{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{86}{\sqrt{38}}{/tex} units
solution: Here,it is given that {tex}\vec{r}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(-9 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Here,we have {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=6 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=-9 i+j-10 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=4 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 4 \\ 4 & 1 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=i(-6-4)-j(12-16){/tex} {tex}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2+4){/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{100+16+36}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{152}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(-9-6) \hat{1}{/tex} {tex}+(1-0) \hat{\jmath}+(6-3) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})={/tex} {tex}(-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=((-10) \times(-15)){/tex} {tex}+(4 \times 1)+(6 \times 3){/tex} = 150 + 4 + 18 = 172 Thus, the shortest distance the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{172}{\sqrt{152}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{172}{2 \sqrt{38}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{86}{\sqrt{38}}{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{86}{\sqrt{38}}{/tex} units
solution_html:
Here,it is given that
{tex}\vec{r}=(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(-9 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
Here,we have
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=6 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=-9 i+j-10 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=4 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\imath} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & -1 & 4 \\ 4 & 1 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=i(-6-4)-j(12-16){/tex}{tex}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2+4){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}=-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{(-10)^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{100+16+36}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{152}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(-9-6) \hat{1}{/tex}{tex}+(1-0) \hat{\jmath}+(6-3) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now,
{tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})={/tex}{tex}(-10 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(-15 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=((-10) \times(-15)){/tex}{tex}+(4 \times 1)+(6 \times 3){/tex}
= 150 + 4 + 18
= 172
Thus, the shortest distance the given lines is
{tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{172}{\sqrt{152}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{172}{2 \sqrt{38}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{86}{\sqrt{38}}{/tex}

Here,it is given equations of lines: 
{tex}{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-6}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-4}{1}{/tex}
{tex}L_2: \frac{x}{-3}=\frac{y+9}{2}=\frac{z-2}{4}{/tex}
Direction ratios of L1 and L2 are (3, -1, 1) and (-3, 2, 4) respectively.
Suppose general point on line L1 is P = (x1, y1, z1)
x1 = 3s + 6, y1 = -s + 7, z1 = s + 4
and suppose general point on line L2 is Q = (x2, y2, z2)
x2 = -3t, y2 = 2t - 9, z2 = 4t + 2
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}{tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}=(-3 t-3 s-6) i+(2 t-9+s-7) \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+(4 t+2-s-4) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=(-3 t-3 s-6) \hat{\imath}+(2 t+s-16)\}{/tex}{tex}+(4 t-s-2) \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((-3t - 3s - 6), (2t + s - 16), (4t - s - 2))
PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines
Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity,
{tex}\implies{/tex}3(-3t - 3s - 60) - 1(2t + s - 16) + 1(4t - s - 2) = 0 and
{tex}\implies{/tex}-3(-3t - 3s - 6) + 2(2t + s - 16) + 4(4t - s - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -9t - 9s - 18 - 2t - s + 16 + 4t - s - 2 = 0 and
9t + 9s + 18 + 4t + 2s + - 32 + 16t - 4s - 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -7t - 11s = 4 and
29t + 7s = -22
Solving above two equations,the we obtain
t = 1 and s = -1
therefore
P = (3, 8, 3) and Q = (-3, -7, 6)
Now, distance between points P and Q is
{tex}d=\sqrt{(3+3)^{2}+(8+7)^{2}+(3-6)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(6)^{2}+(15)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+225+9}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{270}{/tex}
{tex}=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex}
Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}d=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex} units
Now, the equation of the line passing through points P and Q is
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+3}=\frac{y-8}{8+7}=\frac{z-3}{3-6}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-8}{5}=\frac{z-3}{-1}{/tex}

solution: Let {tex}\frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{3}=\lambda{/tex} Coordinate of general point on the given line are {tex}M(5 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+4){/tex} Direction Ratios of PM vector are {tex} \lt 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-2,3 \lambda \gt {/tex} Since, {tex}{P M} \perp \boldsymbol{l}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-2)+3(3 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, coordinates of {tex}M{/tex} are {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{11}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{3}=\lambda{/tex}
Coordinate of general point on the given line are {tex}M(5 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+4){/tex}

Direction Ratios of PM vector are {tex} \lt 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-2,3 \lambda \gt {/tex}
Since, {tex}{P M} \perp \boldsymbol{l}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-2)+3(3 \lambda)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Hence, coordinates of {tex}M{/tex} are {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{11}{2}\right){/tex}

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the given lines. {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}, {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the given lines. {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} , {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Given {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex} Here, we have {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=-2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 6 \\ -2 & 3 & 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}(24-18)-\hat{j}(16{/tex} {tex}+12)+\hat{k}(6-6){/tex} {tex} \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-28 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6^{2}+(-28)^{2}+0^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{36+784+9}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{820}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(3-1) \hat{i}{/tex} {tex}+(3-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-5+4) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}{/tex} Now, we have {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex} {tex}=(6 \hat{\imath}-28 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}=(6 \times 2)+((-28){/tex} {tex}\times 1)+(0 \times(-1)){/tex} = 12 - 28 + 0 = -16 Thus,the shortest distance between the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{-16}{\sqrt{820}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{16}{\sqrt{820}}{/tex} units
solution: Given {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex} Here, we have {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=-2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 6 \\ -2 & 3 & 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}(24-18)-\hat{j}(16{/tex} {tex}+12)+\hat{k}(6-6){/tex} {tex} \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-28 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6^{2}+(-28)^{2}+0^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{36+784+9}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{820}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(3-1) \hat{i}{/tex} {tex}+(3-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-5+4) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}{/tex} Now, we have {tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex} {tex}=(6 \hat{\imath}-28 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}=(6 \times 2)+((-28){/tex} {tex}\times 1)+(0 \times(-1)){/tex} = 12 - 28 + 0 = -16 Thus,the shortest distance between the given lines is {tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{-16}{\sqrt{820}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{16}{\sqrt{820}}{/tex} units
solution_html:
Given
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex}
Here, we have
{tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=-2 \hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 6 \\ -2 & 3 & 8 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}(24-18)-\hat{j}(16{/tex}{tex}+12)+\hat{k}(6-6){/tex}
{tex} \vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-28 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6^{2}+(-28)^{2}+0^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+784+9}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{820}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=(3-1) \hat{i}{/tex}{tex}+(3-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-5+4) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}{/tex}
Now, we have
{tex}(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}}){/tex}{tex}=(6 \hat{\imath}-28 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}=(6 \times 2)+((-28){/tex}{tex}\times 1)+(0 \times(-1)){/tex}
= 12 - 28 + 0
= -16
Thus,the shortest distance between the given lines is

{tex}\mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}) \cdot(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{d}=\left|\frac{-16}{\sqrt{820}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore d=\frac{16}{\sqrt{820}}{/tex} units

question_html: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y-8}{-1}=z-3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+3}{-3}=\frac{y+7}{2}=\frac{z-6}{4}{/tex}
question_text: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y-8}{-1}=z-3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+3}{-3}=\frac{y+7}{2}=\frac{z-6}{4}{/tex}
explanation: Here,it is given that the equation of lines {tex}\mathrm{L} 1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-8}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-3}{1}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{L} 2=\frac{\mathrm{x}+3}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{4}{/tex} Direction ratios of L 1 and L 2 are (3, -1, 1) and (-3, 2, 4) respectively. Suppose general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = 3s + 3, y 1 = -s + 8, z 1 = s + 3 and suppose general point on line L 2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ) x 2 = -3t - 3, y 2 = 2t - 7, Z 2 = 4t + 6 {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(-3 t-3-3 s-3) \hat{i}+{/tex} {tex}(2 t-7+s-8) j+(4 t+6-s-3) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(-3 \mathrm{t}-3 \mathrm{s}-6) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex} {tex}(2 t+s-15) j+(4 t-s+3) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((-3t - 3s - 6, (2t + s - 15), (4t - s + 3)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, {tex}\implies{/tex} 3(-3t - 3s - 6) - 1(2t + s - 15) + 1(4t - s + 3) = 0 and {tex}\implies{/tex} -3(-3t - 3s - 6) + 2(2t + s - 15) + 4(4t - s + 3) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -9t - 9s - 18 -2t - s + 15 + 4t - s + 3 = 0 and 9t + 9s + 18 + 4t + 2s - 30 + 16t - 4s + 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -7t - 11s = 0 and 29t + 7s = 0 Solving above two equations, we get, t = 0 and s = 0 therefore, P = (3, 8, 3) and Q = (-3, -7, 6) Now distance between points P and Q is {tex}d=\sqrt{(3+3)^{2}+(8+7)^{2}+(3-6)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(6)^{2}+(15)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{36+225+9}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{270}{/tex} {tex}=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex} units Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is, {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+3}=\frac{y-8}{8+7}=\frac{z-3}{3-6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{6}=\frac{y-8}{15}=\frac{z-3}{-3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-8}{5}=\frac{z-3}{-1}{/tex} Thus, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-8}{5}=\frac{z-3}{-1}{/tex}

solution_html:
Here,it is given that the equation of lines
{tex}\mathrm{L} 1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-8}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-3}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{L} 2=\frac{\mathrm{x}+3}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{4}{/tex}
Direction ratios of L1 and L2 are (3, -1, 1) and (-3, 2, 4) respectively.
Suppose general point on line L1 is P = (x1, y1, z1)
x1 = 3s + 3, y1 = -s + 8, z1 = s + 3
and suppose  general point on line L2 is Q = (x2, y2, z2)
x2 = -3t - 3, y2 = 2t - 7, Z2 = 4t + 6
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}=(-3 t-3-3 s-3) \hat{i}+{/tex}{tex}(2 t-7+s-8) j+(4 t+6-s-3) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(-3 \mathrm{t}-3 \mathrm{s}-6) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex}{tex}(2 t+s-15) j+(4 t-s+3) \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((-3t - 3s - 6, (2t + s - 15), (4t - s + 3))
PQ will be the shortest distance if it perpendicular to both the given lines
Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity,
{tex}\implies{/tex}3(-3t - 3s - 6) - 1(2t + s - 15) + 1(4t - s + 3) = 0 and
{tex}\implies{/tex}-3(-3t - 3s - 6) + 2(2t + s - 15) + 4(4t - s + 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -9t - 9s - 18 -2t - s + 15 + 4t - s + 3 = 0 and
9t + 9s + 18 + 4t + 2s - 30 + 16t - 4s + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -7t - 11s = 0 and
29t + 7s = 0
Solving above two equations, we get,
t = 0 and s = 0
therefore,
P = (3, 8, 3) and Q = (-3, -7, 6)
Now distance between points P and Q is
{tex}d=\sqrt{(3+3)^{2}+(8+7)^{2}+(3-6)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(6)^{2}+(15)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+225+9}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{270}{/tex}
{tex}=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex}
Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}d=3 \sqrt{30}{/tex} units
Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is,
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+3}=\frac{y-8}{8+7}=\frac{z-3}{3-6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{6}=\frac{y-8}{15}=\frac{z-3}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-8}{5}=\frac{z-3}{-1}{/tex}
Thus, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-8}{5}=\frac{z-3}{-1}{/tex}

question_html: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} are two vectors. The position vectors of the points A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}, respectively. Find the position vector of a point P on the line AB and a point Q on the line CD such that {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} both.
question_text: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} are two vectors. The position vectors of the points A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} , respectively. Find the position vector of a point P on the line AB and a point Q on the line CD such that {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} both.
explanation: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Also, the position vectors of A and C are {tex}6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex} - 9\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} , respectively. Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} . So, P and Q will be foot of perpendicular to both the lines through A and C. Now, equation of the line through A and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} is, {tex}\overrightarrow r = (6\hat i + 7\hat j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} And the line through C and parallel to the vector {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex} is given by {tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} ..... (i) Let {tex}\overrightarrow r = (6i + 7j + 4\hat k) + \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = - 9\hat j + 2\hat k + \mu ( - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k){/tex} .... (ii) Let {tex}P(6 + 3\lambda ,7 - \lambda ,4 + \lambda ){/tex} is any point on the first line and Q be any point on second line is given by {tex}\left( { - 3\mu , - 9 + 2\mu ,2 + 4\mu } \right){/tex} . {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + ( - 9 + 2\mu - 7 + \lambda )\hat j + (2 + 4\mu - 4 - \lambda )\hat k{/tex} {tex} = ( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda )\hat i + (2\mu + \lambda - 16)\hat j + (4\mu - \lambda - 2)\hat k{/tex} If {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} is perpendicular to the first line, then {tex}3( - 3\mu - 6 - 3\lambda ) - (2\mu + \lambda - 16) + (4\mu - \lambda - 2) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 9\mu - 18 - 9\lambda - 2\mu - \lambda + 16 + 4\mu - \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 7\mu - 11\lambda - 4 = 0{/tex} ..... question_text: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x-3}{-1}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{z+2}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+7}{3}=\frac{z+2}{2}{/tex}
explanation: Here, it is given that the equations of a line {tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{1}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{2}{/tex} Direction ratios of L 1 and L 2 are (-1, 2, 1) and (1, 3, 2) respectively Suppose general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = -s + 3, y 1 = 2s + 4, z 1 = s - 2 and let, general points on line L2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ) x 2 = t + 1, y 2 = 3t - 7, z 2 = 2t - 2 {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}=(t+1+s-3) \hat{\imath}+(3 t-7{/tex} {tex}-2 s-4) \hat{\jmath}+(2 t-2-s+2) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(t+s-2) \hat{\imath}+(3 t-2 s-11) \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+(2 t-s) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((t + s - 2), (3t - 2s - 11), (2t - s)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, -1(t + s + 2) + 2(3t - 2s - 11) + 1(2t - s) = 0 and 1(t + s - 2) + 3(3t - 2s - 11) + 2(2t - s) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -t - s + 2 + 2 + 6t - 4s - 22 + 2t - s = 0 and t + s - 2 + 9t - 6s - 33 + 4t - 2s = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7t - 6s = 20 and 14t - 7s = 3s Solving above two equations, we obtain t = 2 and s = -1 thus, P = (4, 2, - 3) and Q(3, -1, 2) Now, distance between points $P$ and $Q$ is {tex}d=\sqrt{(4-3)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(-3-2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(3)^{2}+(-5)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+9+25}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{35}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=\sqrt{35}{/tex} unit Now, the equation of the line passing through points P and Q is, {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-4}{4-3}=\frac{y-2}{2+1}=\frac{z+3}{-3-2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+3}{-5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+3}{5}{/tex} Therefore, equation of the line of the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}\frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+3}{5}{/tex}

solution_html:
Here, it is given that the equations of a line
{tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{2}{/tex}
Direction ratios of L1 and L2 are (-1, 2, 1) and (1, 3, 2) respectively
Suppose general point on line L1 is P = (x1, y1, z1) 
x1 = -s + 3, y1 = 2s + 4, z1 = s - 2
and let, general points on line L2 is Q = (x2, y2, z2)
x2 = t + 1, y2 = 3t - 7, z2 = 2t - 2
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}{tex}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}=(t+1+s-3) \hat{\imath}+(3 t-7{/tex}{tex}-2 s-4) \hat{\jmath}+(2 t-2-s+2) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(t+s-2) \hat{\imath}+(3 t-2 s-11) \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+(2 t-s) \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((t + s - 2), (3t - 2s - 11), (2t - s))
PQ will be the shortest distance if it perpendicular to both the given lines
Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity,
-1(t + s + 2) + 2(3t - 2s - 11) + 1(2t - s) = 0 and
1(t + s - 2) + 3(3t - 2s - 11) + 2(2t - s) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -t - s + 2 + 2 + 6t - 4s - 22 + 2t - s = 0 and 
t + s - 2 + 9t - 6s - 33 + 4t - 2s = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7t - 6s = 20 and
14t - 7s = 3s
Solving above two equations, we obtain
t = 2 and s = -1
thus,
P = (4, 2, - 3) and Q(3, -1, 2)
Now, distance between points $P$ and $Q$ is
{tex}d=\sqrt{(4-3)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(-3-2)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(3)^{2}+(-5)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1+9+25}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{35}{/tex}
Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}d=\sqrt{35}{/tex} unit
Now, the equation of the line passing through points P and Q is,
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-4}{4-3}=\frac{y-2}{2+1}=\frac{z+3}{-3-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+3}{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+3}{5}{/tex}
Therefore, equation of the line of the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}\frac{x-4}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{-3}=\frac{z+3}{5}{/tex}
